segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
LISIE LILIENFELD: Hi.

I'm Lisie Lilienfeld, a product
manager working on Android.

I'm here with my
colleagues, Julie Cattiau
and Casey Burkhardt.

And together, we
are excited to share

some of the latest updates
from Android Accessibility.

For context, we on the
Android accessibility team

follow the principle of
nothing about us without us.

This means that we
innovate not only

for people with disabilities,
but also with them
in collaboration.

So I just want to say
thank you to all the people

with disabilities who were
involved in designing, building,
and testing these features.
OK, let's get into the updates.

First, let me tell you about
some new features for people
with visual impairments.

In this section, I'll
cover updates to TalkBack,

Outline Text, and two features
that are exclusive to Pixel.

People who are blind
or have low vision

are able to use their device
with the assistance of TalkBack,

Android's built-in
screen reader.

It reads out content that
is on the screen in response
to the user's taps and swipes.

Until recently, images
were particularly

hard to understand unless
developers provided
a detailed description.

When the user would
interact with an image,

the descriptions they would get
could be too short or vague.

So an image might be
described as a building
or a view of Sydney, Australia.

But now users can get
image descriptions

powered by Gemini, which are
much more detailed and vivid.

Gemini might say, "The
image shows a wide shot
of Sydney, Australia.

The Sydney Opera House and
the Sydney Harbor Bridge

are visible in the
center of the image.
The sky is clear and blue."

Users with vision impairments
have called these descriptions
a game-changer.

In the past, if they were
browsing social media

or in a group chat, they might
have missed out on the photos.

Even photos in their own camera
roll were hard to experience.

Now TalkBack enables them
to enjoy these photos
of family and friends.

And if you're interested in the
technology behind this feature,

it uses a fast and powerful
server-side Gemini model.

On Pixel 9 Pro Series
devices, TalkBack

can also use the
on-device Gemini Nano

model, which enables users to
get image descriptions even
while offline.

New in this release, we're
extending TalkBack's image

description feature to enable
users to ask Gemini follow-up

questions to get more detail
on what matters to them.

So let's say I'm in a group
chat and a friend shares a photo
of herself with a new guitar.

I might not be able
to see the image,

but I can use TalkBack to get
a description of the image

and then ask Gemini
for more details
specifically about the guitar.

This gives me the context to
respond to my friend's message
with genuine enthusiasm.

Also new, TalkBack
users can now easily

ask Gemini to describe
and answer questions
about the whole screen.

This can help with getting
oriented quickly on a new page,

like the spoken equivalent
of glancing at the screen.

In addition to the
new AI features,

TalkBack has made
several improvements

to help users navigate their
device more efficiently.

Screen Search lets you jump
to what you're looking for,

and now you can search across
multiple pages of an app
within the search results view.

Opening hyperlinks has
also gotten easier,

requiring fewer taps,
even if the link
appears within a block of text.

Finally, TalkBack has also made
it easier to navigate tables
and to use Braille displays.

To learn more about the
new features and TalkBack,

check out the Help Center
link in the description below.
Now on to Outline Text--

designed for people
with low vision,

Outline Text increases
the contrast of the text.

It changes the text to
either black or white,

based on the
original text color,

and adds a subtle,
transparent highlight
behind the text for contrast.

One nice detail is
that it preserves

the color of links
and colored text

so users won't miss out
on a color that indicates
important information.

For people who
have Pixel devices,

there are two more
features designed
for vision accessibility.

Pixel Magnifier helps
people with low vision
to see the world around them.

It uses the Pixel camera to
let people capture and zoom in

on anything they want
to see more clearly.

New updates include the ability
to search for specific words

within an image and
picture in picture,

so you can keep track of
where you are in an image when
you zoom way in.
Finally, there's Guided Frame.

Guided Frame helps Pixel
users capture great photos

by giving spoken feedback
on what's in the viewfinder.

New in Guided Frame is improved
object detection and guidance

on taking selfies
at a good angle.

Guided Frame turns
on automatically

when a user has
TalkBack turned on.

But now it can also
be enabled directly
in the Pixel camera settings.

And if you want to revisit
all the photos you've

captured using
Guided Frame, you can

use TalkBack to hear
vivid descriptions

and ask Gemini to remind
you of the details from all
your favorite moments.

Now I'll pass it
over to Julie to talk
about hearing accessibility.
JULIE CATTIAU: Thank you, Lisie.
Hi, my name is Julie.

I'm a product manager on the
Android accessibility team.

And I'd love to
share with you what

we've been working on for
the deaf and hard-of-hearing
community.

The first update I
have is a feature
called Expressive Captions.

You may already be using
Live Captions, which

is designed to caption any
video you watch on your Android
device.

Expressive Captions
is a new feature

in Live Captions that
helps viewers experience
the emotions in a video.

In order to do that,
Expressive Captions

include captions that convey
emphasis, tone, and sounds.

And with Expressive Captions V2,
which is coming in Android 16,

we'll also be adding
word duration,

as featured in this video
clip, and new sounds,

such as when someone is
whispering or yawning.

Next, we've made some
improvements to Live Transcribe.

Live Transcribe is
an app that captions

real-world, face-to-face
conversations
in more than 80 languages.

You can use Live
Transcribe today anywhere,

even if you're offline,
in 15 languages that
are available on device.

We've recently added a
handy dual-screen mode

for foldable phones, making
face-to-face conversations even
easier.

And in June, we'll also
add support for pinch

to zoom so our users can
easily and quickly increase

the font size in
Live Transcribe.
Moving on to hearing aids--

we've made it easier
to directly transfer
a call to your hearing aid.

You can now directly select your
hearing aid from the phone app

while you're making
a phone call.

This feature is available
for all hearing aids devices
that are LE audio supported.

We're also making it easier
for users of hearing aids

to access key hearing
aid settings directly
from the Quick Settings menu.

Starting in Android
15, users could easily

manage hearing aid settings
like presets, battery level,

and Bluetooth from
the shortcut menu.

And building on this,
in Android 16, users

will also be able to view their
hearing aids' connection status

and control their hearing
aid ambient volume,
all in one place.

Let's now take a look at
some of our updates focused
on dexterity.

We recently introduced new
keyboard accessibility features

for devices with connected
physical keyboards.

Mouse keys allows users to
control the on-screen mouse

pointer using the keypad buttons
instead of a mouse or trackpad.

This feature can be useful for
users with limited hand or arm

movement, who may find
it difficult to use
a traditional mouse.

The Sticky Keys feature allows
you to press shortcut keys one

at a time instead of having
to press multiple buttons
simultaneously.

Slow Keys allows you to adjust
how long you need to hold down
a key before it's registered.

And finally, Bounce Keys
ignores quickly repeated presses
of the same key.

All these features aim to
improve typing and navigation

for users with
limited dexterity,

and we hope you'll
find them helpful.

And with that, I'll
pass it over to Casey

to talk about developer
and API updates.
CASEY BURKHARDT: Thanks, Julie.
Hi.
My name is Casey Burkhardt.

I'm a software engineer on the
Android accessibility team.

I'm excited to
highlight some changes

to the accessibility
APIs, as well as updates

to accessibility best practices
and tooling for app developers.

The Android accessibility
API is the foundation

that allows apps
to expose their UI

and functionality to a wide
range of assistive technologies.

And in Android 16, we've
made a number of additions

to the platform
accessibility API

to allow apps to more
expressively represent
their semantics.

We'll touch on a few
of important additions,

particularly in the
AccessibilityNodeInfo

and AccessibilityEvent
classes, but you

can find the full details
on developer.android.com.

You should also check out this
year's Build More Accessible UIs

with Jetpack Compose
Google I/O session

to learn about the
accessibility APIs

through the semantics
surfaced by Compose-based UI.

We've added a new concept known
as a supplemental description,

which offers a structured way to
add an additional accessibility

label to UI elements in order
to describe their purpose.

Supplemental descriptions
are particularly

useful when defined on
containers or focusable
groupings of UI.

They should be
used in cases where

you want to expose some
additional information

about the group in a way
that won't override the text
or description from its subtree.

You can think of
supplemental descriptions

as a complement to content
descriptions, which

offer similar labeling
functionality but will

override the descriptions
from the group's descendants.

This API allows you to augment
how a screen reader would

describe your UI without having
to build up your own content

descriptions, which
can be error prone

or introduce challenges
with localization.

As an example, a
supplemental description

might be used on
a custom dropdown

menu that doesn't otherwise have
a label describing its purpose.

In this case, we use font_family
to convey that information

to users while preserving other
text labels that represent
the currently selected font.

We've also added XML
attributes and APIs on view

to make it more
convenient to provide
supplemental descriptions.

The accessibility API now
offers a structured way

to represent a UI element's
expanded or collapsed state.

Previously, the
best approach was

to surface this information
through the free-form state

description property,
but we now recommend
migrating to this new API.

AccessibilityNodeInfo's
new expandedState property

allows you to convey
whether something on screen

is collapsed, partially,
or fully expanded.

And the new expanded
content change type

on AccessibilityEvent
signals any change

in that UI's expanded state
to accessibility services.

When UI can be expanded
and collapsed by users,

you should continue
to support and respond

to action expand and action
collapse accessibility actions,

which are supported
going back to API 21.

We've also added a
way for developers

to convey partially
checked controls
within the accessibility API.

You'll sometimes
encounter this pattern

in hierarchical lists, where
a checkbox for a parent group

uses this state to represent
that some of its children

are selected while
others are not.

We've deprecated
AccessibilityNodeInfo's Boolean

checked property and replaced
it with an integer property that

can represent true, false,
or partial checked states.

We've also added a new
content change type

on AccessibilityEvent to convey
a change in a UI element's
checked state.

Previously, sending a
TYPE_VIEW_CLICKED accessibility

event from a checkable
element was expected.

We still recommend dispatching
that event on earlier API

versions, but you should begin
using this new approach starting

at API 36 to ensure
screenreaders provide feedback

when the checked state
of a control changes.

Another addition to the
accessibility API in Android 16

relates to how apps represent
form fields that require
user input or selection.

A common practice is to indicate
required or optional fields

using a visible text
label, sometimes

relying on an asterisk to denote
which fields require input.

In addition to
visible labels, we now

also recommend using
AccessibilityNodeInfo's

new fieldRequired property
to convey this information

to Accessibility Services
in a structured way.

Previously, there was not
a consistent way for apps

to indicate to
Accessibility Services

that a UI element was displaying
an indeterminate state,

such as loading
indicators and progress

bars without a numerical
progress value.

In Android 16, we've
made an addition

to AccessibilityNodeInfo's
Range Info class

to offer a reliable way
to convey this state.

When displaying such
a progress indicator,

we recommend using the new
indeterminate RangeInfo

type on the accessibility node
info emitted by that element.

You can continue to set a
content description or state

description too if you'd like
to convey additional information
to users.

Next up, I'd like to highlight
some recently updated
accessibility best practices.

As you heard from
Lisie, we're continuing

to improve TalkBack's image
description features, which

automatically offer detailed
information about images
and photos.

Developers play
an important role

in ensuring this feature
works well across apps.

We continue to
recommend providing

your own descriptive and
localized accessibility

labels throughout
your UIs. But in cases

where that's not
possible, such as where

images from user-generated
content are displayed,

there's another important
step in ensuring these image
description features work well.

Conveying image semantics
from UI elements that

may benefit from
additional descriptions

will cue TalkBack to offer
or automatically generate
image descriptions.

When using Compose,
this is accomplished

by using or extending
an image Composable.

If you're working with views,
use an ImageView, ImageButton,
or one of their subclasses.

If you're using a
custom view, override

getAccessibilityClassName and
return android.widget.ImageView.

As we continue to evolve
Android's Accessibility API,

we're focusing on offering
app developers the ability

to convey a richer
set of UI semantics

that generalize to a wider
range of assistive technologies.

We're also moving away
from APIs and practices

that are tied to the behaviors
of specific accessibility
services.

Last year, we strongly
discouraged the use

of accessibility
announcements, a concept that

often overoptimizes the
experience for users of screen

readers and may produce
inconsistent, sometimes
disruptive experiences.

With Android 16,
we're officially

deprecating the APIs used to
generate these announcements.

And we're planning to end
support for TalkBack's handling
of them in a future release.

To make it easier
to test for this,

TalkBack's speech
output overlay,

which you can enable from
its developer settings,

will turn red when
producing feedback

from accessibility
announcements.

If your app relies
on TYPE_ANNOUNCEMENT

AccessibilityEvents or view's
announceForAccessibility method,

now is the time to migrate to
a more semantic alternative.

These alternatives include
providing descriptive labels

that clearly communicates
actions associated

with UI elements,
eliminating the need

to provide confirmation
announcements for most user

interactions; using window
and pane titles to convey

a high-level description for
activities and fragments,

which are described by
TalkBack automatically

during transitions; or defining
live regions on UI elements

that appear temporarily or
update their descriptions

if users should be made aware of
those related content changes.

For more details,
check out the announce

for accessibility API
documentation linked
from the description below.
Finally, an update on testing--

we've heard many
requests from developers

for accessibility checks that
work from composed tests.

And today I'm excited to
share that in Compose 1.8,

we've released an integration
between Android's Accessibility

Test Framework and the
Compose UI test library.

This new offering can
leverage existing tests

using Android Compose
test rule to identify

some common accessibility
issues automatically.

To enable these
checks, add the UI test

JUnit 4 accessibility
dependency,

call the enable accessibility
checks extension

function on Android
Compose test rule,

and trigger a test action, such
as calling perform click OK.

You can also invoke try
perform accessibility checks

to explicitly run the
accessibility checks
at any point during a test.

If the accessibility
test framework

identifies a potential
issue, it will throw
an exception failing the test.

You can customize the behavior
of the accessibility checks

by providing your own
AccessibilityValidator instance.

This allows you to do things
like configure the severity

level for problems that will
cause tests to fail or provide

a matcher for suppressing
failures from known issues.

We continue to strongly
recommend developers test apps

by using them with Android's
assistive technologies

to best understand the
experience users will encounter.

These automated checks
complement manual testing

by accelerating the
detection of a variety

of possible
accessibility barriers.

We've covered a lot
here, from updates

to accessibility features
related to vision, hearing,

and dexterity to changes
in our APIs, best
practices, and tooling.

Be sure to check out the
links in the description

to learn more
about these topics.

Thanks for joining us today
and for doing your part

to build an ecosystem of highly
accessible and inclusive apps.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
